metadata.summary: DDD Repository pattern: abstract interface in the domain layer returns Aggregates by identity; infrastructure implements persistence. No ORM types in the domain interface.

content.title: DDD Repository Pattern: Domain-Owned Persistence Interfaces
content.summary: A Repository provides a collection-like interface for accessing Aggregates. The domain layer defines the interface (abstract class with find_by_id, save, delete); the infrastructure layer provides the ORM implementation. The domain never imports ORM types. The Repository returns fully reconstituted Aggregates — never raw ORM models — and is responsible for translating between domain objects and persistence models.

why.p: Without Repositories, domain logic imports SQLAlchemy sessions, Django ORM queries, or JPA EntityManagers directly. This couples the domain to the database: changing the database technology requires changing domain code. It also makes unit testing impossible without a real database.

The Repository pattern inverts the dependency: the domain defines what it needs (find_by_id, save), and the infrastructure delivers it. Domain unit tests use in-memory fake Repositories; infrastructure tests verify the SQLAlchemy implementation against a real DB.

list.item: Any Aggregate that must be persisted and reloaded across requests or transactions.
When you want to unit-test domain logic without a running database.
When the domain layer must remain ORM-agnostic (to support multiple backends or future migrations).
Systems where the query side and write side diverge: Repository handles write-side Aggregate loading; a separate Read Model handles query-side projections.
Simple CRUD apps where the ORM model IS the domain model — a thin Active Record is faster and produces no value in return for the indirection.
Read-side projections and reporting queries — those go in a Query Service or Read Model, not a Repository.
Throwaway scripts or ETL pipelines where ORM is used directly and domain concepts do not apply.

rule.statement: The Repository interface MUST live in the domain layer and MUST NOT import any ORM or infrastructure types (no SQLAlchemy, Django ORM, JPA, EF). It MUST declare methods using only domain types: find_by_id(UUID) -> Optional[Aggregate], save(Aggregate) -> None, delete(Aggregate) -> None.
rule.rationale: ORM types in the domain interface couple the domain to the infrastructure. Swapping the ORM then requires changing domain code, which is the dependency inversion violation the Repository is designed to prevent.

rule.statement: The Repository MUST return fully reconstituted Aggregate objects, never ORM model instances. The infrastructure implementation is responsible for translating between persistence models and domain objects via _to_entity() / _to_model() methods.
rule.rationale: Returning ORM models leaks persistence metadata (lazy-loading proxies, change-tracking) into the domain. Domain code then depends on ORM lifecycle semantics instead of domain semantics.

rule.statement: Repositories MUST load Aggregates by identity only (find_by_id). Collection queries by arbitrary criteria MUST go in a separate Read Model or Query Service, never in the Repository interface.
rule.rationale: Adding rich query methods to the Repository blurs the boundary between the write model (Aggregates) and the read model (projections). It also encourages bypassing Aggregate invariants by loading partial state.

example.title: Domain interface + SQLAlchemy implementation
example.note: The domain interface uses only domain types. _to_entity() reconstructs the full Aggregate from the persistence model — the domain never sees ORM proxies.

example.title: ORM types leaking into the domain interface
example.note: Importing Session in the domain couples it to SQLAlchemy. Returning an ORM model leaks lazy-loading behaviour into domain code.

section.name: in-memory-fake
section.p: Provide an in-memory fake Repository for unit tests. This makes domain tests fast (no DB) and deterministic.

example.title: In-memory fake repository for unit tests
example.code:
# tests/fakes.py from typing import Dict, List, Optional from uuid import UUID from order.domain.repositories import OrderRepository from order.domain.entities.order import Order
class InMemoryOrderRepository(OrderRepository): def __init__(self): self._store: Dict[UUID, Order] = {}
async def find_by_id(self, order_id: UUID) -> Optional[Order]: return self._store.get(order_id)
async def find_by_customer(self, customer_id: UUID) -> List[Order]: return [o for o in self._store.values() if o.customer_id == customer_id]
async def save(self, order: Order) -> None: self._store[order.id] = order
async def delete(self, order: Order) -> None: self._store.pop(order.id, None)

section.name: agentic-gotchas
section.p: Agents often add rich query methods to the Repository (find_by_status, find_active, get_recent), turning it into an ORM wrapper. This pattern must be caught in review.

list.item: Verify the domain interface imports no ORM types — agents frequently import Session or EntityManager in the abstract class.
Reject find_by_* methods beyond find_by_id — those belong in a Read Model or Query Service.
Check _to_entity() reconstructs a full Aggregate, not just a shallow copy — missing nested entities create partially hydrated Aggregates.
Agents may skip _to_entity() entirely and return ORM model instances — this leaks SQLAlchemy proxy semantics into the domain.

checklist.check: Repository interface lives in domain layer with no ORM imports
Interface methods use only domain types (UUID, Order, Optional[Order])
Infrastructure implementation has _to_entity() that returns a fully reconstituted Aggregate
No collection query methods (find_by_status, etc.) on the Repository — those go in Read Models
In-memory fake Repository exists for unit testing
Events are dispatched after the DB transaction commits, not inside save()
